title: Isaiah 16
subtitle: The Oracle Against Moab (Part 2)

heb.paragraph: "In love a throne (kissēʾ) will be established; in faithfulness a man will sit on it — one from the house of David — one who in judging seeks justice and speeds the cause of righteousness" (v.5). Moab is counselled to send tribute to Zion (v.1), to shelter Judah’s fugitives (v.3–4). If Moab shows mercy to refugees, the messianic throne of justice will be their protection (v.5).

refs.note: He will reign on David’s throne with justice and righteousness — the same messianic throne.
A shoot from Jesse’s stump judging with righteousness — the same messianic figure.
David’s great-grandmother was Ruth the Moabite — the Moabite connection to David’s throne.
A ruler from Bethlehem — another messianic throne-promise in a judgment context.

notes.note: Send tribute lambs to Zion. Moab’s survival path: alliance with God’s people.
Shelter our fugitives; do not betray the refugees. The appeal for asylum — Israel asking Moab for mercy.
The messianic throne: established in love, seated in faithfulness, judging with justice. The Davidic hope inserted into a foreign oracle.
Calvin reads the refugee-asylum request as evidence that Isaiah foresees Judah’s own suffering: before Moab falls, Judah will need Moab’s help. The oracle is not triumphalist but realistic.
Calvin: the Davidic throne appearing in a Moab oracle universalises the messianic hope. The throne of justice is not just for Israel but for any nation that aligns with it.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes the tribute of lambs connects to Moab’s pastoral economy. Mesha king of Moab paid 100,000 lambs annually to Israel (2 Kgs 3:4). The tribute system was well established.

The NET observes four qualities of the messianic ruler: love (chesed), faithfulness (ʾĕmeth), justice (mishpat), righteousness (tsedaqah). The same vocabulary as Isa 1’s indictment, now fulfilled in the king.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt reads the asylum-offer as the oracle’s most distinctive feature: in the midst of judgment, the prophet offers Moab a survival strategy. Judgment is not God’s only word — mercy is always available to those who respond.
Oswalt: the messianic throne in a foreign oracle confirms Isaiah’s universal vision. The Davidic king is not Israel’s private possession but the world’s righteous ruler.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs reads the passage as a canonical testimony to the interconnection of judgment and hope: even within an oracle of doom, the messianic promise surfaces. The canonical Isaiah refuses to let judgment have the last word.
Childs notes the vocabulary (love, faithfulness, justice, righteousness) matches the covenant attributes of YHWH himself. The messianic king governs with God’s own character.

hist.context: The messianic promise embedded in a judgment oracle (v.5) is striking: even in the oracle against Moab, the Davidic hope appears. The counsel to shelter refugees (vv.3–4) implies a period when JUDAH is in distress and Moab could help. The "house of David" reference connects to 9:7 and 11:1–10. The Moab oracle’s structure is unique: instead of pure condemnation, it offers Moab a path to survival through alliance with Zion.

sections.header: Verses 6–14 — “My Heart Laments for Moab Like a Harp”

heb.paragraph: "My heart laments (hāmâ) for Moab like a harp" (v.11). Moab’s pride is reported: great is her arrogance (v.6). Therefore Moab wails for her raisin cakes; the terraces of Heshbon wither; the grapevines of Sibmah (vv.7–8). "I drench you with tears, Heshbon and Elealeh" (v.9). No songs in the vineyards (v.10). Isaiah’s own heart moans like a harp’s strings vibrating in sympathy (v.11). Moab’s prayers on the high places accomplish nothing (v.12). "Within three years Moab’s splendour will be despised" (v.14).

refs.note: Jeremiah laments for Moab using the same vineyard imagery and many of the same phrases — directly borrowing from Isa 16.
My heart cries out over Moab — the parallel expression of prophetic grief from the previous chapter.
Jesus weeps over Jerusalem — the same prophetic grief for a people facing judgment.
I take no pleasure in the death of the wicked — the theological principle behind Isaiah’s tears.

notes.note: Moab’s pride and arrogance. The diagnosis before the death sentence.
"I drench you with tears." No songs in the vineyards. No shouting at harvest. The prophet weeps over the enemy’s lost joy.
My heart laments like a harp. The harp-string image: the prophet’s insides vibrate with sympathetic grief.
Within three years. The timeline given. Specific enough to test.
Calvin reads the prophet’s tears as modelling the divine heart: God grieves over the judgment he enacts. Calvin sees this as the corrective to any theology that celebrates destruction.
Calvin notes the three-year deadline is a mercy: it gives Moab time to respond. The timeline is not a threat but a window.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes Sibmah’s vine "reached as far as Jazer, wandered to the desert, its shoots spread abroad, crossed over the sea." This describes the extensive trade reach of Moab’s wine industry, now destroyed.
The NET observes "my inward parts" (meʿay) is literally "my intestines/bowels" — the seat of deep emotion in Hebrew. The prophet’s grief is visceral, not cerebral.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt reads the tears-amid-judgment as the oracle’s theological centre: God’s prophets do not relish destruction. The capacity to weep over the enemy is a mark of prophetic authenticity.
Oswalt: the harp image is profound — the prophet’s soul vibrates in resonance with Moab’s pain. Empathy is not agreement; it is shared suffering. Isaiah grieves for Moab while announcing Moab’s fall.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs reads the Moab oracle as a canonical model for how to hold judgment and compassion together: the prophet announces doom and weeps simultaneously. The canon teaches readers to do the same.

Childs notes the three-year addition (v.14) grounds the oracle in specific history. The canonical text contains both the eternal principle (pride falls) and the temporal fulfilment (within three years).

hist.context: The prophet’s empathy reaches its peak: "I drench you with tears" (v.9) and "my heart laments like a harp" (v.11). No other judgment oracle contains this degree of prophetic grief. The vineyard imagery (vv.8–10) reflects Moab’s actual economy: the Sibmah and Heshbon regions were famous wine-producing areas. The three-year deadline (v.14) is specific enough to be verifiable — possibly referring to an Assyrian campaign that devastated Moab within that timeframe.

ppl.name: king
ppl.text: 5) is striking: even in the oracle against Moab, the Davidic hope appears…

ppl.name: Israel
ppl.text: Moab is appealing to Judah for asylum and protection, not Israel asking Moab for mercy.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV\u201cMake up your mind,\u201d Moab says. \u201cRender a decision. Make your shadow like night\u2014 at high noon. Hide the fugitives, do not betray the refugees.ESV\u201cGive counsel; grant justice; make your shade like night at the height of noon; shelter the outcasts; do not reveal the fugitive;NIVLet the Moabite fugitives stay with you; be their shelter from the destroyer.\u201d The oppressor will come to an end, and destruction will cease; the aggressor will vanish from the land.ESVlet the outcasts of Moab sojourn among you; be a shelter to them from the destroyer. When the oppressor is no more, and destruction has ceased, and he who tramples underfoot has vanished from the land,

src.title: The Cyrus Cylinder (539 BC)
src.quote: The Cyrus Cylinder relates to Deutero-Isaiah (chapters 40-55), not Isaiah chapter 1 being discussed here.
src.note: The cylinder confirms Isaiah’s prophecy of a Persian king who would liberate captives and authorize temple rebuilding, whether the prophecy is dated early (8th c.) or late (6th c.).

src.title: The Sennacherib Prism (c.700 BC)
src.quote: The Sennacherib Prism relates to events described in Isaiah 36-39, not Isaiah chapter 1.
src.note: The silence confirms Isaiah 36–37: Sennacherib besieged but never conquered Jerusalem. His own records inadvertently verify Isaiah’s narrative of divine deliverance.

src.title: The Great Isaiah Scroll (1QIsaᵃ, Dead Sea Scrolls)
src.quote: A complete copy of Isaiah found at Qumran, dated c.125 BC. The oldest complete biblical manuscript, it is virtually identical to the Masoretic text 1,000 years later.
src.note: The scroll demonstrates the remarkable textual stability of Isaiah across a millennium. The text the church reads today is essentially the text the Qumran community read.

rec.text: "Send tribute lambs to Zion. Moab’s survival path: alliance with God’s people."

rows.label: The Prophet Weeps — 16:6–14
rows.text: Pride of Moab. "I DRENCH you with tears." "My heart laments like a HARP." Within three years.

hebtext.note: "In love a throne (kissēʾ) will be established; in faithfulness a man will sit on it — one from the house of David — one who in judging seeks justice and speeds the cause of righteousness" (v.5). Moab is counselled to send tribute to Zion (v.1), to shelter Judah’s fugitives (v.3–4). If Moab shows mercy to refugees, the messianic throne of justice will be their protection (v.5).

"My heart laments (hāmâ) for Moab like a harp" (v.11). Moab’s pride is reported: great is her arrogance (v.6). Therefore Moab wails for her raisin cakes; the terraces of Heshbon wither; the grapevines of Sibmah (vv.7–8). "I drench you with tears, Heshbon and Elealeh" (v.9). No songs in the vineyards (v.10). Isaiah’s own heart moans like a harp’s strings vibrating in sympathy (v.11). Moab’s prayers on the high places accomplish nothing (v.12). "Within three years Moab’s splendour will be despised" (v.14).

thread.text: He will reign on David’s throne with justice and righteousness — the same messianic throne.
A shoot from Jesse’s stump judging with righteousness — the same messianic figure.
David’s great-grandmother was Ruth the Moabite — the Moabite connection to David’s throne.
A ruler from Bethlehem — another messianic throne-promise in a judgment context.
Jeremiah laments for Moab using the same vineyard imagery and many of the same phrases — directly borrowing from Isa 16.
My heart cries out over Moab — the parallel expression of prophetic grief from the previous chapter.

themes.note: Mercy peaks at 8: the prophet weeps for the enemy. Justice at 7: the messianic throne governs with righteousness.

coaching.tip: 'In faithfulness a throne will be established; in the tent of David one will sit on it' (v. 5). Buried inside the Moab oracle is a messianic promise — the Davidic king who judges with justice. Isaiah weaves messianic hope into the least expected contexts. Even oracles of judgment contain seeds of restoration. The reader trained to spot this pattern finds Christ foreshadowed everywhere.

'My heart laments for Moab like a harp' (v. 11). Isaiah compares his grief to a musical instrument — judgment expressed as elegy. The prophet's emotions are not incidental to the message but part of it. God doesn't deliver judgment with clinical detachment. The divine pathos — God's own grief over what sin requires — runs through Isaiah like a bass note.

prayer_prompt: As Moab is told to establish a throne founded on faithfulness, consider asking God whether the decisions you are making right now are building a legacy of steadfastness or expedience.
